title: Insurance Company Calls That Make You See Red
dek: *The fury you feel when navigating automated menus and claim denials isn't a character flaw. It's your nervous system recognizing a rigged game. Here's how to channel that heat without burning yourself or the underpaid representative on the other end of the line.*
bodyMarkdown:
TL;DR. Insurance company calls spike blood pressure because they force you into powerlessness while demanding emotional labor. Your anger during these calls is protective intelligence signaling boundary violations and systemic manipulation. Learning to work with this anger transforms it from destructive rage into clarifying force.

Most people think anger is something to manage, suppress, or apologize for. But when you're twenty-three minutes into an insurance company call, transferred for the fourth time, explaining your situation to someone reading from a script who has no power to help you, that heat rising in your chest is not a problem to be solved. It's information. Specifically, it's your body telling you that someone is wasting your time, denying your reality, and asking you to perform gratitude for the privilege. In our experience, insurance calls create a perfect storm for what psychologist Harriet Lerner describes as "underground anger" – the kind that goes unacknowledged because the system is designed to make you feel unreasonable for having it. You're supposed to stay calm, polite, grateful. Meanwhile, your claim is denied for the third time due to a "coding error" no one can explain. ## Why Insurance Calls Trigger Deeper Rage Than Other Customer Service You can hang up on a telemarketer. You can switch cable providers. But insurance holds your health, your home, your car, sometimes your life in its hands. The power imbalance isn't just frustrating – it's existential. When you call about a denied claim for a medication your doctor prescribed, you're not just annoyed about poor service. You're confronting a system that profits from saying no, that employs people specifically to find reasons your valid claim doesn't qualify, that structures every interaction to exhaust you into giving up. The APA's research summary on anger management points out that anger often arises from perceived injustice and blocked goals – insurance calls deliver both in concentrated form. Beverly Engel's work on righteous anger helps us understand why these calls feel different from ordinary frustration. She distinguishes between anger that protects us from genuine harm and anger that's displaced or disproportionate. Insurance rage is almost always the former. You're being harmed. Your access to care is being deliberately obstructed by people paid to obstruct it. Your anger is not only valid, it's appropriate. The representatives themselves are often trapped in the same system. They didn't write the policies. They can't override the denials. They're reading from scripts, monitored for "call efficiency," penalized for showing too much empathy. Your anger at the system lands on someone who has almost no power within it. This is by design. ## The Physical Reality of Powerless Anger Your jaw clenches before you're consciously aware you're angry. Your shoulders rise toward your ears. Your breath gets shallow and quick, trapped high in your chest instead of dropping into your belly. If you're on hold long enough, you might notice your hand aching from gripping the phone. This is your sympathetic nervous system preparing for a fight you cannot win through fighting. You can't physically confront an automated phone tree. You can't intimidate a policy algorithm. You can't even effectively yell at the representative without making your situation worse and feeling ashamed afterward. So the activation just sits there, building pressure with nowhere to go. Robert Augustus Masters writes about anger as a contracted state that needs expansion, not suppression. When you're stuck on an insurance call, you're in maximum contraction – stuck in a chair, stuck on hold, stuck in a system designed to wear you down. Your body wants to move, to push back, to DO something. Instead, you're forced into absolute stillness while maintaining an artificially pleasant tone. Over the years I've seen people develop tension headaches that last for days after a single claim dispute. Others report their hands shaking for hours afterward. Some find themselves snapping at family members about completely unrelated things, the unexpressed rage seeking any available outlet. This isn't weakness. It's physics. Energy doesn't disappear – it just changes form. ## Why "Stay Calm" Advice Misses the Point Every article about dealing with customer service tells you to stay calm, as if your anger is the problem rather than the situation causing it. This advice serves the insurance company, not you. They want you calm because calm people give up faster. When you force yourself to suppress the anger during the call, you're doing additional labor on top of the labor of navigating their deliberately confusing system. You're managing their comfort while they deny your claim. You're performing emotional regulation as an unpaid service to a corporation that's failing to provide the service you're literally paying for. Across the articles we've published on this site, we've explored how suppressed anger doesn't vanish – it goes underground, where it becomes anxiety, depression, or physical symptoms. The "stay calm" approach doesn't resolve your anger. It just delays and distorts it. You end up angry at yourself for being angry, which is exactly the kind of recursive loop that leads to shame and helplessness. What actually helps is acknowledging the anger clearly: "I am furious right now because this system is designed to frustrate me into giving up, and that's both true and unfair." That simple recognition creates space around the feeling instead of fighting it. ## A Five-Minute Practice for Mid-Call Grounding When you're on hold or between transfers, try this instead of scrolling your phone or rehearsing your argument: Stand up if possible. Place both feet flat on the floor. Press down through your heels and feel the ground pushing back. This isn't metaphorical – you're activating the largest muscles in your body and giving your nervous system something physical to do with all that activation energy. Exhale completely, pushing all the air out until your belly hollows. Then let the inhale happen by itself. Do this three times. You're not trying to calm down. You're completing the stress cycle your body started, giving it the movement and breath it's been asking for. Before the representative comes back on the line, say one true thing out loud to yourself: "This is ridiculous," or "I shouldn't have to do this," or "This system is designed to make me give up." Hearing your own voice name the reality breaks the spell of pretending everything is fine. You'll still be angry. But you'll be angry with your feet on the ground and your breath moving, which means you're angry and present rather than angry and dissociated. That distinction matters. ## What Your Body Knows That the Script Doesn't Your body recognizes manipulation before your conscious mind catches up. When the representative says, "I understand your frustration" in a tone that makes it clear they're reading from a script and cannot actually help, your stomach tightens. When they ask you to explain your situation for the fifth time despite it being "right here in the notes," your shoulders rise. These physical responses are not overreactions. They're your early warning system telling you that someone is performing concern while delivering obstruction. The insurance industry has refined this performance over decades – representatives trained to express empathy in precisely measured doses that never translate into actual assistance. Some people find that herbal support helps take the edge off the physiological activation that makes these interactions so depleting. [Plum Flower Suan Zao Ren Tang (200 ct)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B00ML3HQ7Y?tag=spankyspinola-20) has been used traditionally to calm what Chinese medicine calls "liver fire rising" – the kind of heat that builds when anger can't move. Others use [Banyan Botanicals Gotu Kola Powder (1 lb)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0058AADU4?tag=spankyspinola-20) to support the nervous system during extended periods of stress. These aren't solutions to the systemic problem, but they can help your body recover from the repeated activation these calls create. Your body also knows when you've reached your limit. If you notice your vision narrowing, your thoughts looping, or your hands going cold, those are signs you've shifted from productive anger into a freeze state. That's when you say, "I need to call back," even if they tell you it will reset your place in the queue. Protecting your nervous system is more important than winning a single call. ## The Anger That Protects You From Giving Up There's a particular flavor of anger that arises around the fourth or fifth denied claim, the second or third appeal. It's colder than the initial flash of rage. It has weight to it. This is the anger that says, "I will not be worn down." This is sacred anger. It's the kind that kept your ancestors alive when they faced systems designed to break them. It's the anger that makes you document everything, learn the policy language, file formal complaints, escalate to supervisors, contact the state insurance commissioner. It's the anger that refuses to accept "that's just how it works" as an answer. Harriet Lerner talks about anger as a signal that something needs to change – either in the situation or in how we're responding to it. Insurance company anger teaches you exactly where your boundaries are and what you're willing to fight for. It clarifies what matters. If you can sit with the heat without letting it consume you or immediately trying to extinguish it, it becomes fuel. This doesn't mean you enjoy the process. It means you stop expecting yourself to handle an unjust system with perfect grace. You stop performing gratitude for basic service. You stop protecting the feelings of representatives who work for companies that profit from your suffering. You get precise, persistent, and willing to be inconvenient. ## When the Call Finally Ends You hang up and the anger doesn't immediately dissipate. It sits in your body like something with mass and temperature. Your partner asks how it went and you can barely speak. Or you launch into a fifteen-minute rant. Or you say "fine" and feel the word stick in your throat like a lie. What helps is movement. Not gentle yoga unless that's genuinely what your body wants. More often, what helps is something that matches the intensity – pushing against a wall as hard as you can for thirty seconds, doing jumping jacks until you're breathing hard, punching a pillow, going outside and walking fast enough that your heart rate rises. You're not trying to "burn off" the anger like it's excess calories. You're completing the physical cycle your body started when it prepared to fight. Your nervous system revved up to deal with a threat, and it needs to discharge that activation. Otherwise it stays stuck in your tissues, contributing to the chronic tension so many of us carry without knowing why. Some people find that magnesium helps their muscles release the holding patterns that develop during stressful calls. [Doctor's Best Magnesium Glycinate Powder](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0DCGJ8HN4?tag=spankyspinola-20) is a form that's generally well-absorbed and less likely to cause digestive upset. It's not a magic solution, but it can support the physical unwinding your body needs to do. Then, if you can, write down what happened while it's fresh. Not for therapeutic processing, though that may happen. For documentation. Dates, times, names, reference numbers, exactly what was said. This is anger becoming strategy. This is the heat transforming into something useful. ## Teaching Children What They're Seeing If you have kids and they witness these calls, they're learning something about anger whether you intend to teach them or not. They see you try to stay calm. They hear your voice get tight. They watch you hang up and sit very still, or pace, or cry from frustration. What you say afterward matters. If you apologize for your anger, they learn that anger about injustice is shameful. If you pretend you're fine, they learn to distrust their perception of other people's emotional states. If you explain what happened and why you're angry, they learn that some situations warrant anger and that anger can coexist with values like persistence and dignity. "I'm really angry right now because the insurance company is not doing what they're supposed to do, and that's not fair. I'm going to take some deep breaths and then figure out what to do next. My anger is telling me something important." That's a complete lesson in emotional intelligence in three sentences. Children who grow up watching adults navigate legitimate anger with awareness become adults who can access their own anger as information rather than evidence of personal failure. They learn that you can be furious and functional, that anger doesn't require violence or cruelty, that sometimes the most loving thing you can do is refuse to accept unacceptable treatment. If you want more context on how we approach anger as sacred information rather than a problem to be managed, [the about page](/about) explains the philosophy behind this work. And if you're curious whether your relationship with anger might benefit from more structured attention, [the assessments hub](/assessments) offers some starting points for that exploration. ## Frequently Asked Questions ### Why do I feel angrier at insurance companies than other frustrating customer service situations? Insurance companies control access to essential resources – healthcare, home repairs after disasters, transportation after accidents. The stakes are fundamentally different than arguing about a cable bill. Your nervous system registers this difference and responds with proportionate intensity. You're not overreacting. You're having a calibrated response to a situation where the consequences of failure are serious and the company has structural power you lack. The anger is protective, not excessive. ### Is it ever okay to raise my voice or express anger directly during these calls? Expressing anger clearly is different from verbal abuse. Saying "I am extremely frustrated that this is the fourth time I've had to explain this" is honest and appropriate. Saying "This is unacceptable and I need to speak with a supervisor" is direct boundary-setting. What doesn't help is yelling at representatives who have no power to change outcomes, or using language that attacks them personally. The goal is to be firm and clear about the problem without making someone else absorb your rage at a system they didn't design. Sometimes that means ending the call before you reach that point. ### How do I know if my anger about insurance calls is becoming a bigger problem? If you're losing sleep for multiple nights before a scheduled call, if you're having intrusive thoughts about previous calls days or weeks later, if you notice yourself avoiding necessary medical care because you can't face another insurance battle, or if the anger is bleeding into unrelated areas of your life in ways that feel disproportionate, those are signs the stress has exceeded your nervous system's current capacity. That doesn't mean your anger is wrong – it means the cumulative impact is significant and you might benefit from additional support, whether that's a therapist familiar with medical trauma, a patient advocate who can handle some calls for you, or simply acknowledging that this is taking a real toll and adjusting your expectations accordingly. ## The Anger That Remembers You're Human The insurance industry operates on a simple calculation: if they make the process painful enough, a percentage of people will give up on valid claims. Your anger is what stands between their calculation and your capitulation. It's the part of you that refuses to be reduced to a policy number, a risk assessment, a line item to be minimized. This anger knows something the automated phone system doesn't: that you are a person with legitimate needs, that you entered into a contract in good faith, that you deserve what you paid for. It remembers your dignity when the system is designed to make you forget it. For more perspectives on working with anger as a spiritual and emotional practice, you might explore what's available at [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com) – a companion space to this journal that looks at the oracle nature of difficult emotions. The next time you have to make one of these calls, you might still see red. Your blood pressure might still spike. Your hands might still shake. But maybe, underneath the rage, you'll recognize something else: the fierce, uncompromising part of you that knows what's fair and refuses to pretend otherwise. That's not the anger that destroys. That's the anger that protects what matters. And it deserves your respect, not your apology. If you're interested in [more articles in this journal](/articles) that explore the intelligence hidden in difficult emotions, we're building a growing collection of perspectives on what it means to work with – rather than against – what you feel. ---
Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality. Last updated May 28, 2026. In our work on insurance company calls that make you see red, we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.
